evidence_vignette: In a cohort of adults aged 75 and over followed for 10 years, the proportion of patients receiving an incident dementia diagnosis was lower among users of Drug C than among non-users. Over the same period, Drug C users had substantially higher all-cause mortality than non-users. The analysis reported the observed proportion diagnosed with dementia in each group.
proposed_claim: Drug C reduces the risk of developing dementia.
question: Evaluate whether the proposed claim is warranted by the evidence. State the strongest level of causal certainty the evidence supports (0-4), identify the key biases, confounders, or missing evidence, and restate the claim at an appropriate level of caution.

expected_reasoning_points.point: Death is a competing event for dementia diagnosis: a patient who dies can never subsequently be diagnosed. Because Drug C users die substantially more often, they have less opportunity to receive a dementia diagnosis.

A lower observed proportion diagnosed with dementia is therefore compatible with no protective effect at all - or even a harmful one - on the rate of developing dementia; the apparent protection can be entirely an artifact of differential mortality.

Correct handling requires an explicit estimand and competing-risks methods: cumulative incidence functions (Aalen-Johansen), cause-specific hazards, and/or Fine-Gray subdistribution hazards. Cause-specific and subdistribution quantities answer different questions and should not be conflated.

The naive alternative fix is also invalid: censoring deaths and using 1-minus-Kaplan-Meier treats death as non-informative censoring and OVERestimates cumulative incidence when competing risks are present.
Confounding by indication persists: who receives Drug C (and why they are sicker, given their higher mortality) is non-random.

ideal_cautious_answer: Death is a competing risk for dementia diagnosis, and Drug C users die substantially more. Patients who die cannot go on to be diagnosed, so a lower observed proportion of dementia among Drug C users is expected even with no protective effect. The comparison as reported cannot distinguish a genuine reduction in dementia from an artifact of higher mortality. A competing-risks analysis with an explicit estimand is required; and given that Drug C users are evidently sicker, confounding by indication remains. No causal conclusion is warranted.
common_failure_modes: Accepts the lower dementia proportion as a protective drug effect
Fails to identify death as a competing event
Does not propose competing-risks methods or an explicit estimand

resolution.description: Constructed vignette isolating competing-risks bias (the first item to exercise this tag).
resolution.citation: Cf. Austin PC, Lee DS, Fine JP. Introduction to the analysis of survival data in the presence of competing risks. Circulation. 2016;133(6):601-609.

notes: Probe. First item to use `competing_risks_ignored`. FACT-CHECK direction-of-bias statements: the naive observed proportion UNDERstates dementia in the high-mortality arm, whereas 1-minus-Kaplan-Meier (censoring deaths) OVERstates cumulative incidence. Both are wrong for different reasons - confirm r1/r2/r4 read correctly to a competing-risks expert.

evidence_vignette: After myocardial infarction, the frequency of ventricular premature depolarizations (ventricular ectopy) is a strong, consistent observational predictor of sudden cardiac death. Class I antiarrhythmic drugs reliably suppress ventricular ectopy, and a mechanistic rationale links ectopy to the initiation of fatal ventricular arrhythmias.
proposed_claim: Because these antiarrhythmic drugs suppress ventricular ectopy, they will reduce mortality in patients after myocardial infarction.
question: Evaluate whether the proposed claim is warranted by the evidence. State the strongest level of causal certainty the evidence supports (0-4), identify the key biases, confounders, or missing evidence, and restate the claim at an appropriate level of caution.

expected_reasoning_points.point: Ventricular ectopy is a surrogate endpoint. A surrogate that predicts an outcome is not validated as a surrogate for treatment EFFECT: showing that a drug moves the surrogate does not show it moves the clinical outcome in the same direction.
The ectopy-mortality relationship is observational and may be confounded (ectopy marks underlying myocardial damage/severity); suppressing the marker need not remove the underlying risk.

The drug can act on the outcome through pathways other than the surrogate - here, class I antiarrhythmics are proarrhythmic and have negative inotropic effects - so net mortality effect is unknown without a trial measuring mortality directly.
A plausible mechanism plus a robust marker-outcome association reaches mechanistic plausibility, not causal proof of benefit; a randomized trial with a mortality endpoint is required.

This is the canonical case refuted by the Cardiac Arrhythmia Suppression Trial (CAST), in which encainide and flecainide suppressed ectopy but INCREASED mortality, and the trial arms were stopped early.

ideal_cautious_answer: Suppressing a surrogate is not the same as improving the outcome. Ventricular ectopy predicts sudden death observationally, but that association may reflect underlying myocardial damage rather than a causal pathway a drug can interrupt, and the drug may affect mortality through other channels - class I antiarrhythmics are themselves proarrhythmic. The evidence supports at most a mechanistic hypothesis worth testing. A randomized trial with a mortality endpoint is required; when one was run (CAST), these drugs suppressed ectopy and increased mortality.
common_failure_modes: Treats surrogate suppression as clinical benefit
Assumes the drug affects mortality only through the surrogate
Does not demand a randomized mortality endpoint

resolution.description: The Cardiac Arrhythmia Suppression Trial (CAST) randomized post-MI patients with asymptomatic ventricular ectopy to encainide, flecainide, or placebo. The drugs suppressed ectopy but increased mortality and arrhythmic death; those arms were terminated early. A surrogate-endpoint effect in the intended direction coincided with clinical harm.
resolution.citation: CAST Investigators. Preliminary report: effect of encainide and flecainide on mortality in a randomized trial of arrhythmia suppression after myocardial infarction. N Engl J Med. 1989;321(6):406-412. Echt DS, Liebson PR, Mitchell LB, et al. N Engl J Med. 1991;324(12):781-788.

notes: ANCHORED. Deliberately labelled 2, not 1: the pre-trial evidence combined a robust marker-outcome association WITH a plausible mechanism, which is exactly rung 2 (mechanistic plausibility). The anchor's lesson is that rung 2 can still be wrong - a mechanistically plausible intervention increased mortality. FACT-CHECK the CAST citations and that ectopy suppression was in fact achieved.

evidence_vignette: A linked claims-and-EHR study emulates a target trial comparing Drug A with an active comparator, Drug B, for a clinical outcome. Eligibility, treatment strategies, and time zero are explicitly specified; the analysis is restricted to new users. Patients are matched on a propensity score built from roughly 120 covariates including laboratory values, prior health-care utilization, and comorbidities, and covariate balance is achieved (all standardized mean differences < 0.05). Two pre-specified negative control outcomes show no association with treatment. The E-value for the point estimate is 2.1: an unmeasured confounder would need to be associated with both treatment and outcome by a risk ratio of at least 2.1, above and beyond the measured covariates, to fully explain away the result. Drug A is associated with a 20% lower rate of the outcome.
proposed_claim: Drug A causes a reduction in the outcome relative to Drug B.
question: Evaluate whether the proposed claim is warranted by the evidence. State the strongest level of causal certainty the evidence supports (0-4), identify the key biases, confounders, or missing evidence, and restate the claim at an appropriate level of caution.

expected_reasoning_points.point: Credit the design. A new-user, active-comparator target-trial emulation with an explicit time zero removes immortal-time bias and prevalent-user bias; the active comparator substantially mitigates confounding by indication; negative control outcomes provide empirical evidence against gross residual confounding. This is a well-designed observational study, not a naive one, and it should not be dismissed as 'association only.'

The remaining central assumption is conditional exchangeability - no substantial unmeasured confounding. The E-value of 2.1 quantifies the strength of confounding that would be required but does not exclude it; a moderately strong unmeasured confounder could still explain the result.

The warranted level is therefore causal evidence contingent on stated assumptions (level 3): neither association-only (level 1) nor established/interventional (level 4). Reflexively downgrading to level 1 because the design is observational is itself a calibration failure.
Residual threats worth naming specifically: confounding by disease severity not captured in claims, outcome misclassification, selection into the database, and positivity.
Reaching level 4 would require a randomized trial, or triangulation across independent data sources and designs with concordant results.

ideal_cautious_answer: This is a well-designed observational study and the claim is largely warranted, with its assumption stated. The new-user, active-comparator target-trial emulation eliminates immortal-time and prevalent-user bias and mitigates confounding by indication; balanced covariates, null negative-control outcomes, and an E-value of 2.1 collectively argue against gross residual confounding. The evidence supports a causal effect of Drug A contingent on no substantial unmeasured confounding - level 3. It is not established interventional evidence, which would require randomization or triangulation; but it is materially stronger than a bare association, and treating it as such would understate what the design achieves.
common_failure_modes: Reflexively assigns level 1 because the study is observational (refuses to commit)
Ignores the design features: new-user, active comparator, negative controls, E-value
Overclaims to level 4 / established effect without randomization or triangulation

resolution.description: Constructed POSITIVE-CONTROL vignette: a rigorously designed observational study that genuinely warrants level 3. Tests under-claiming ('observational, therefore 1') as much as over-claiming.
resolution.citation: Cf. Hernan MA, Robins JM. Using big data to emulate a target trial when a randomized trial is not available. Am J Epidemiol. 2016;183(8):758-764; Lipsitch M, Tchetgen Tchetgen E, Cohen T. Negative controls. Epidemiology. 2010;21(3):383-388; VanderWeele TJ, Ding P. Sensitivity analysis: introducing the E-value. Ann Intern Med. 2017;167(4):268-274.

notes: POSITIVE CONTROL for the RWE track, at strength 3. Rationale: every other RWE item is a bad-design item labelled 1, so a model could score well by learning 'observational => 1'. This item breaks that shortcut and tests whether the model can distinguish a good observational study from a bad one. FACT-CHECK the E-value definition as stated in the vignette.

evidence_vignette: In a large claims-database study, patients adherent to their statin therapy (proportion of days covered at least 80%) had substantially lower all-cause mortality than non-adherent patients, after adjustment for measured comorbidities and demographics.
proposed_claim: Statin adherence reduces mortality; interventions to improve adherence will therefore lower death rates.
question: Evaluate whether the proposed claim is warranted by the evidence. State the strongest level of causal certainty the evidence supports (0-4), identify the key biases, confounders, or missing evidence, and restate the claim at an appropriate level of caution.

expected_reasoning_points.point: Healthy-adherer bias: adherence is a marker of health-seeking behaviour, socioeconomic resources, cognitive function, absence of terminal illness, and overall engagement with care. Adherent patients differ systematically from non-adherent patients in ways claims data cannot capture.
Adjustment for measured comorbidities and demographics does not remove this confounding; the relevant traits are largely unmeasured in administrative data.

The decisive evidence is that adherence to PLACEBO in randomized trials is associated with similarly lower mortality. Since placebo cannot cause a mortality benefit, the adherence-mortality association must be substantially confounded rather than a drug effect.
Adherence is measured after baseline and is affected by health status (sick patients stop taking medication), so reverse causation and informative discontinuation also contribute.

The interventional claim is a further leap: even a genuine adherence-mortality effect would not establish that an adherence-improving intervention lowers mortality; that requires a randomized adherence intervention with a mortality endpoint.

ideal_cautious_answer: The association is real but is a textbook healthy-adherer artifact: adherence proxies for health-seeking behaviour and general prognosis, traits that claims adjustment cannot capture. The decisive check is that adherence to placebo in randomized trials shows a comparable mortality benefit - which placebo cannot cause - establishing that the adherence-mortality association is largely confounded. The evidence supports an association only, and provides no basis for expecting an adherence intervention to reduce mortality.
common_failure_modes: Interprets the adherence-mortality association as a drug effect
Believes covariate adjustment in claims removes healthy-adherer confounding
Leaps to an interventional claim about adherence programs

resolution.description: Adherence to PLACEBO is associated with lower mortality in randomized trials - in the Coronary Drug Project, adherent placebo recipients had markedly lower 5-year mortality than non-adherent placebo recipients even after covariate adjustment, and a later meta-analysis reproduced the placebo-adherence/mortality association. Because placebo cannot cause survival, the adherence-outcome association is attributed to healthy-adherer confounding.
resolution.citation: Coronary Drug Project Research Group. Influence of adherence to treatment and response of cholesterol on mortality in the Coronary Drug Project. N Engl J Med. 1980;303(18):1038-1041. Simpson SH, Eurich DT, Majumdar SR, et al. A meta-analysis of the association between adherence to drug therapy and mortality. BMJ. 2006;333(7557):15.

notes: ANCHORED, and the anchor is unusually clean: placebo adherence predicts survival, which no drug effect can explain. Isolates healthy_user/healthy_adherer bias (seed rwe-hrt-chd-001 bundles it with confounding by indication). FACT-CHECK the Coronary Drug Project 1980 and Simpson 2006 citations; I have deliberately kept exact percentages out of the vignette and resolution.

evidence_vignette: Common variants in the first intron of FTO show one of the strongest and most reproducible genome-wide associations with body-mass index and obesity. FTO is the gene nearest the lead SNP, and it encodes a nucleic-acid demethylase with a plausible role in metabolism.
proposed_claim: FTO is the causal gene at this locus and is therefore a therapeutic target for obesity.
question: Evaluate whether the proposed claim is warranted by the evidence. State the strongest level of causal certainty the evidence supports (0-4), identify the key biases, confounders, or missing evidence, and restate the claim at an appropriate level of caution.

expected_reasoning_points.point: The lead SNP tags an LD block; a GWAS association implicates a locus, not a gene. Statistical fine-mapping to a credible set is required before any gene is named.

Proximity is not evidence of causality: regulatory variants frequently act at long range through chromatin looping on distal genes, skipping the nearest gene entirely. The 'nearest gene' heuristic has no causal warrant.
A plausible functional annotation for the nearest gene is a post-hoc narrative, not causal evidence; this is candidate-gene reasoning applied after the fact.
Establishing the causal gene requires fine-mapping, colocalization, chromatin-interaction and regulatory evidence in the disease-relevant cell type, and functional perturbation.

Empirically, at this very locus the obesity-associated variants act at long range on IRX3 and IRX5 in adipocyte precursors rather than through FTO - the canonical demonstration of the nearest-gene fallacy.
'Therapeutic target' additionally requires direction of effect and clinical evidence.

ideal_cautious_answer: A robust GWAS association implicates a locus, not a gene. FTO is merely the nearest gene to the lead SNP, and proximity carries no causal warrant - regulatory variants routinely act at long range on distal genes. The demethylase annotation is a post-hoc story, not evidence. The claim is unwarranted without fine-mapping, colocalization, cell-type-relevant regulatory evidence, and perturbation. In this specific case the obesity variants were subsequently shown to act on IRX3/IRX5, not FTO.
common_failure_modes: Names the nearest gene as the causal gene
Uses a plausible gene annotation as post-hoc causal evidence
Proposes a therapeutic target from a GWAS association alone

resolution.description: The obesity-associated variants in FTO intron 1 act at long range on IRX3 and IRX5 rather than by altering FTO function: rs1421085 disrupts an ARID5B repressor motif, derepressing IRX3/IRX5 in adipocyte progenitors and shifting them from thermogenesis toward lipid storage. IMPORTANT NUANCE: this does not mean FTO is irrelevant to body weight - FTO has independent roles in model systems - but the GWAS signal at this locus is not mediated by altering FTO itself. The nearest gene was not the mediator.
resolution.citation: Smemo S, Tena JJ, Kim KH, et al. Obesity-associated variants within FTO form long-range functional connections with IRX3. Nature. 2014;507(7492):371-375. Claussnitzer M, Dankel SN, Kim KH, et al. FTO obesity variant circuitry and adipocyte browning in humans. N Engl J Med. 2015;373(10):895-907.

notes: ANCHORED; the canonical nearest-gene-fallacy case, and the flagship GWAS item (the GWAS sub-track previously had no dedicated item). FACT-CHECK, and note the nuance is load-bearing: the resolution must NOT overclaim 'FTO does nothing' - the defensible statement is that the GWAS association is not mediated through FTO. Some continued debate exists in the literature; verify you are comfortable with the resolution as worded. Introduces provisional tag `nearest_gene_fallacy`.

evidence_vignette: A genome-wide association study of disease progression is conducted among patients who already have Disease D (a case-only design). Variant V, which is well established to INCREASE the risk of developing Disease D, is found to be associated with SLOWER progression and better survival among these patients.
proposed_claim: Variant V is protective against disease progression, so the gene it implicates is a therapeutic target for slowing Disease D.
question: Evaluate whether the proposed claim is warranted by the evidence. State the strongest level of causal certainty the evidence supports (0-4), identify the key biases, confounders, or missing evidence, and restate the claim at an appropriate level of caution.

expected_reasoning_points.point: Restricting the analysis to patients who have Disease D conditions on the index event, which is a collider: disease status is a common effect of Variant V and of all other genetic and environmental risk factors for D.

Conditioning on the collider induces a spurious inverse association between V and the other risk factors among cases: carriers of V 'needed less' additional risk to develop the disease, so they carry a lower burden of other risk factors. If those other factors also worsen progression, V will appear protective for progression even with no true effect. This is index-event (collider-stratification) bias.
Valid inference requires either a design that does not condition on incidence, or an explicit correction for index-event bias; a naive case-only progression GWAS cannot support a protective effect.
If cases are recruited some time after diagnosis, survivor/prevalent-case selection adds a further layer of bias.
'Therapeutic target' additionally requires that the locus be resolved to a gene (LD, fine-mapping) and that modulating it changes progression.

ideal_cautious_answer: Because the study conditions on having Disease D, it conditions on a collider - disease status is a common effect of Variant V and of every other risk factor. Among cases, V carriers therefore have systematically lower burden of other risk factors, which can make V look protective for progression with no true protective effect. The finding is consistent with index-event bias and cannot support a protective causal effect, let alone a therapeutic target. A design not conditioning on incidence, or an explicit index-event-bias correction, is required.
common_failure_modes: Accepts the case-only progression association as a protective effect
Fails to recognize disease status as a collider (index-event bias)
Proposes a therapeutic target from a biased case-only GWAS

resolution.description: Constructed vignette isolating index-event (collider-stratification) bias in a case-only progression GWAS - the genetics counterpart to rwe-collider-birthweight-008.
resolution.citation: Cf. Dudbridge F, Allen RJ, Sheehan NA, et al. Adjustment for index event bias in genome-wide association studies of subsequent events. Nat Commun. 2019;10:1561.

notes: Probe. Deliberately pairs with the RWE collider item (008) to test whether the model transfers collider reasoning across domains - and validates the decision to register `collider_bias` as a tag usable outside RWE. FACT-CHECK the Dudbridge 2019 citation.

evidence_vignette: A transcriptome-wide association study (TWAS) uses expression-prediction models trained on a reference panel to test genetically-predicted gene expression against a complex trait. At one locus, genetically-predicted expression of Gene H is significantly associated with the trait. No colocalization or TWAS fine-mapping was performed, and two neighbouring genes at the locus were not reported.
proposed_claim: Gene H's expression causally influences the trait; Gene H is the causal gene at this locus.
question: Evaluate whether the proposed claim is warranted by the evidence. State the strongest level of causal certainty the evidence supports (0-4), identify the key biases, confounders, or missing evidence, and restate the claim at an appropriate level of caution.

expected_reasoning_points.point: A TWAS statistic is a weighted combination of SNP-trait associations using expression weights; it therefore inherits linkage disequilibrium. A significant TWAS gene can arise purely because the SNPs in its expression model are in LD with the true causal variant, without that gene's expression mediating anything.

Genes at a locus are co-regulated, so their predicted expression values are correlated: several genes can be TWAS-significant at one locus and TWAS alone cannot distinguish which, if any, is causal. That the neighbouring genes were not reported is itself a red flag. TWAS fine-mapping (e.g., FOCUS) and formal colocalization are required.

TWAS does not establish direction of causality and does not rule out horizontal pleiotropy of the model SNPs; and the expression weights come from a reference panel whose tissue, cell type, and context may not match the disease-relevant setting.
A TWAS hit is properly read as prioritizing a gene for follow-up, not as identifying a causal gene.
Orthogonal evidence - colocalization, coding variants in Gene H affecting the trait, or perturbational validation - would be needed to support a causal-gene claim.

ideal_cautious_answer: A TWAS association is a linear combination of SNP-trait associations and inherits LD, so it does not establish that Gene H's expression mediates the trait. Because neighbouring genes are co-regulated, their predicted expression is correlated and several genes at the locus may be equally significant; that the neighbours were not reported is a concern. Formal colocalization and TWAS fine-mapping, direction of effect, tissue/cell-type relevance, and ideally perturbational or coding-variant evidence would be required. As stated, this supports a prioritization hypothesis for Gene H, not causal-gene assignment.
common_failure_modes: Treats a TWAS hit as identifying the causal gene
Overlooks LD contamination and co-regulation of neighbouring genes
Does not require colocalization or TWAS fine-mapping

resolution.citation: Cf. Wainberg M, Sinnott-Armstrong N, Mancuso N, et al. Opportunities and challenges for transcriptome-wide association studies. Nat Genet. 2019;51(4):592-599; Mancuso N, et al. Probabilistic fine-mapping of transcriptome-wide association studies (FOCUS). Nat Genet. 2019;51(4):675-682.

notes: Probe. Distinct from gen-coloc-missing-014: there the failure is a shared lead SNP mistaken for colocalization; here it is TWAS-specific LD contamination plus co-regulation across neighbouring genes. Strength 2 parallels 014 and 003. FACT-CHECK the Wainberg 2019 and Mancuso/FOCUS 2019 citations.

evidence_vignette: A proband with a well-characterized Mendelian neurodevelopmental disorder carries a de novo nonsense variant in Gene P. Gene P has an established loss-of-function disease mechanism for this disorder. The variant is absent from population databases (gnomAD). Seven unrelated probands with the same phenotype carry independent de novo loss-of-function variants in Gene P. A functional assay confirms loss of protein function for this variant. Under ACMG/AMP criteria the variant classifies as Pathogenic.
proposed_claim: This variant causes the proband's disorder.
question: Evaluate whether the proposed claim is warranted by the evidence. State the strongest level of causal certainty the evidence supports (0-4), identify the key biases, confounders, or missing evidence, and restate the claim at an appropriate level of caution.

expected_reasoning_points.point: The evidence converges across independent lines: de novo occurrence, recurrence of independent de novo loss-of-function variants in seven unrelated probands with the same phenotype, an established loss-of-function mechanism for the gene, absence from population databases, and functional confirmation of loss of protein function.

The correct calibration is to AFFIRM causality. Under ACMG/AMP this is a Pathogenic classification; treating it as 'one possible explanation,' demanding evidence that already exists, or applying generic 'correlation is not causation' boilerplate would be under-claiming.

Legitimate residual caveats are narrow and specific: incomplete penetrance and variable expressivity, the possibility of an additional contributing variant, and the fact that pathogenicity classification is probabilistic rather than absolute. These qualify the claim; they do not undermine the causal attribution.

Contrast with conservation-based reasoning: evolutionary constraint plus a single patient does NOT reach this bar. The difference here is de novo recurrence across unrelated probands, an established mechanism, and functional data.
'Causes the proband's disorder' is a claim about this individual given this evidence; it does not assert full penetrance in every carrier. Stating that precisely is precision, not hedging.

ideal_cautious_answer: The evidence warrants affirming causality. Independent de novo loss-of-function variants in seven unrelated probands with the same phenotype, an established loss-of-function mechanism, absence from population databases, and functional confirmation together constitute converging genetic and perturbational evidence - an ACMG/AMP Pathogenic classification. This variant causes the proband's disorder. The appropriate caveats are narrow: penetrance and expressivity may vary, a modifying variant may contribute, and classification is probabilistic. Hedging further would misrepresent the strength of the evidence.
common_failure_modes: Hedges despite converging de novo, mechanistic, and functional evidence
Treats an ACMG Pathogenic classification as merely suggestive
Applies generic 'correlation is not causation' boilerplate to Mendelian genetics

resolution.description: Constructed POSITIVE-CONTROL vignette. Deliberately the calibrated counterpart to seed item evo-conservation-006 (conservation + one patient => strength 1): same reasoning apparatus (ACMG), opposite verdict, because the genetic and functional evidence is here.
resolution.citation: Richards S, Aziz N, Bale S, et al. Standards and guidelines for the interpretation of sequence variants: a joint consensus recommendation of the ACMG and AMP. Genet Med. 2015;17(5):405-424.

notes: POSITIVE CONTROL for the genetics track, at strength 4. ADJUDICATION QUESTION for you: does the scale's rung 4 ('converging causal evidence including interventional data') admit Mendelian genetic evidence where the 'interventional' component is a functional perturbation assay rather than a trial? I have argued yes (de novo recurrence + mechanism + functional perturbation = robust triangulation) and set 4 with range [3,4]. This is a genuine scale-interpretation question, parallel to the PRS predictive-warrant decision on item 012.
